aliases: rice vermicelli in peanut satay sauce

english: rice vermicelli in peanut satay sauce

food_subgroup: Cooked rice vermicelli (bee hoon) topped with a thick peanut-based satay sauce, blanched cockles, fishcake, cuttlefish, and bean sprouts

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no concept for Satay Bee Hoon. Closest parent is noodle dish with peanut sauce (FOODON:03315595 — noodle dish). No exact URI available; null used to preserve ontology honesty.
ontology.foodex2_description: Composite dish — noodles with sauce and mixed protein toppings (closest FoodEx2 parent: mixed noodle dish). Used as closest defensible parent; no exact FoodEx2 code exists for Satay Bee Hoon.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for Satay Bee Hoon. A0GXM (composite dish — noodles) used as closest defensible parent anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for Satay Bee Hoon or its closest parent in the clinical food hierarchy.

allergens: gluten (possible — soy sauce in satay sauce)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard hawker plate of Satay Bee Hoon weighs approximately 450 g, comprising roughly 150 g rice vermicelli, 150 g peanut satay sauce, and 150 g mixed toppings (cockles, fishcake, cuttlefish, bean sprouts, kangkong).

nutrient_source.title: Satay Bee Hoon — Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Satay Bee Hoon. HPB Foodo is the primary approved source for Singapore hawker dishes. Core macros and micronutrients are reported as verified (confidence 1.0). Copper, manganese, and vitamin K are reported from enriched_json cross-reference (confidence 0.9); these are consistent with USDA FoodData Central component values for peanut-based mixed dishes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — rice vermicelli and peanut-based composite dish estimation
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Satay Bee Hoon. Composite GI estimated at ~65 (medium) based on: (1) rice vermicelli GI 58–70 per University of Sydney GI Database (closest analogous staple); (2) peanut sauce component (high fat, high protein, high fibre) lowers composite GI relative to plain rice vermicelli; (3) seafood protein toppings further moderate postprandial glucose response. GL per serving calculated as: GI 65 × available carbohydrate per serving (72g per 450g plate) / 100 = 46.8. This is a high glycemic load per serving driven primarily by the large rice vermicelli portion.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 320 mg per 100 g; approximately 1,440 mg sodium per standard 450 g serving (72% of WHO 2,000 mg daily limit), contributed by shrimp paste (belacan), soy sauce in satay sauce, and processed fishcake.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Satay Bee Hoon to occasional consumption and request reduced sauce quantity to lower sodium intake per serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load (~46.8 GL per 450 g serving) driven by a large rice vermicelli portion (GI ~65, ~72 g available carbohydrate per plate); peanut satay sauce partially moderates glucose response via fat and protein but does not offset the large carbohydrate load.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid regular consumption of Satay Bee Hoon; if consuming, request a reduced bee hoon portion, extra vegetables, and less sauce to lower the glycemic load per meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (2.0 g per 100 g; ~9 g per serving) from peanut satay sauce and coconut milk; cholesterol 45 mg per 100 g (~202 mg per serving) from cockles and cuttlefish; sodium 1,440 mg per serving elevates cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume Satay Bee Hoon occasionally and in smaller portions, noting that the peanut-derived monounsaturated fat and vitamin E provide partial cardiovascular benefit, but the high sodium and cholesterol load from seafood toppings and processed fishcake warrant moderation.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Peanut satay sauce provides resveratrol, monounsaturated fat, and vitamin E (1.8 mg per 100 g); cockles contribute iron and vitamin B12 as anti-inflammatory cofactors.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Satay Bee Hoon is generally suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to the anti-inflammatory profile of peanut-derived nutrients, though sodium content should be monitored.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Satay Bee Hoon (沙爹米粉, Shātē Mǐfěn) is a uniquely Singaporean hawker dish with no direct equivalent anywhere else in the world. It consists of blanched rice vermicelli noodles (bee hoon) generously coated in a thick, complex peanut satay sauce and topped with blanched cockles (hum), sliced fishcake, cuttlefish, bean sprouts, and morning glory (kangkong). Despite the name 'satay', this dish contains no grilled meat skewers — the name refers exclusively to the satay-style peanut sauce. The dish is believed to have originated in Singapore's Jurong area in the 1950s–1960s, created by Teochew-Chinese hawkers who adapted the Malay satay peanut sauce concept to a noodle format, producing a harmonious fusion of Chinese and Southeast Asian culinary traditions. The peanut satay sauce is both the dish's defining flavour and its primary nutritional concern: it is rich, nutty, mildly spicy, and deeply savoury-sweet, made from roasted peanuts, dried chillies, lemongrass, shrimp paste (belacan), and spices. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides 152 kcal, 7 g protein, 7.5 g fat, and 16 g carbohydrate. A standard 450 g hawker plate delivers approximately 684 kcal, 31.5 g protein, 33.75 g fat, and 72 g carbohydrate. The glycemic load per serving is high (~46.8), driven by the large rice vermicelli portion. The seafood toppings (cockles, cuttlefish, fishcake) contribute meaningful vitamin B12 (0.8 mcg per 100 g), iron (1.5 mg), zinc (1.5 mg), and selenium (12 mcg). Sodium is moderate-to-high at 320 mg per 100 g (~1,440 mg per serving), sourced from belacan, soy sauce, and processed fishcake. The dish is dairy-free but contains major allergens including peanuts, shellfish, fish, and cephalopods. It is not suitable for halal diets due to the presence of cockles and potential cross-contamination. Clinically, Satay Bee Hoon is high concern for Type 2 Diabetes due to its high glycemic load, moderate concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease due to sodium and cholesterol content, and generally low concern for rheumatoid arthritis due to the anti-inflammatory peanut and seafood nutrient profile.
common_user_queries: satay bee hoon peanut sauce nutrition

confidence_reason: Core macros and most micronutrients sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo, the primary approved source for Singapore hawker dishes, with confidence 1.0. Copper, manganese, and vitamin K sourced from enriched_json cross-reference at confidence 0.9, consistent with USDA FoodData Central component values for peanut-based mixed dishes. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database (approved GI source) with clearly documented estimation basis. Overall record confidence is high.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for Satay Bee Hoon not separately reported in HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of April 2026. 2. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this composite hawker dish not reported in HPB Singapore Foodo database. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo source). 4. GI is an analogous estimate (GI ~65, medium) based on rice vermicelli GI from University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Satay Bee Hoon exists in any approved source. Glycemic load per 450 g serving = 46.8 (high). 5. FoodEx2 code A0GXM used as closest defensible parent (composite noodle dish); no exact FoodEx2 code exists for Satay Bee Hoon. FoodOn URI set to null — no exact FoodOn concept exists. 6. Copper (0.12 mg), manganese (0.2 mg), and vitamin K (45 mcg) values are from enriched_json cross-reference at confidence 0.9; these are plausible for a peanut-based noodle dish with seafood toppings per USDA FoodData Central component data. 7. Hindi local name (सताय बी हून) is a phonetic transliteration; no established Hindi culinary term exists for this dish. 8. The dish is not halal-certified due to the presence of cockles (shellfish) and potential use of non-halal-certified ingredients in hawker preparation. 9. Portions: the primary serving size is 450 g (1 plate = 150 g bee hoon + 150 g peanut sauce + 150 g toppings) per HPB Foodo; duplicate 400 g entries in input have been consolidated and the 450 g verified portion is used as default.
